commonFlags.description: Interface to capture on. -i any captures all interfaces (Linux only).
Write raw packets to file (pcap) instead of decoding to stdout.
Read packets from a saved pcap file instead of the wire.
Don't resolve addresses — keeps output fast and avoids leaking the analyst's IP via reverse DNS.
Don't resolve addresses or service names (port 80 stays 80, not http).
Increasing verbosity in the per-packet decode.
Stop after capturing N packets — keeps quick checks bounded.
Snapshot length. Default is 262144 on modern tcpdump; set 0 for full packets, smaller for headers-only.
Print packet payloads as ASCII — useful for plaintext protocols (HTTP, FTP, IRC).
Hex + ASCII dump alongside the decode.
Print the link-layer header (MAC addresses, VLAN tags).
Print timestamps in absolute date-time form, not relative offsets.
Rotate the output file every N seconds (used with -w).
Rotate the output file once it reaches N megabytes.
Limit the number of rotated files to keep — older ones are overwritten.
Drop privileges to <user> after opening the capture handle.

bpfFilters.description: Anything to or from a specific host
Only packets originating from that host
Only packets destined to that host
Anything on port 53 (DNS), TCP or UDP
A whole port range — handy for app debugging
TCP setup and teardown packets only
Exclude your own SSH session from the capture

recipes.title: Quick "is traffic reaching me?" check
recipes.details: Hard-stops after 20 packets, no DNS lookups, focused on one host:port. The fastest way to confirm or rule out connectivity from the host's point of view.

recipes.title: Capture for offline Wireshark analysis
recipes.details: Full packets (-s 0) so payloads aren't truncated, written to a pcap file. Copy off the box, open in Wireshark.

recipes.title: Long-running capture with ring buffer
recipes.details: Rotates after 100 MB or 1 hour, keeps the last 10 files. Ring-buffering keeps disk usage bounded for unattended captures, and -G timestamps each file.

recipes.title: DNS query log
recipes.details: -l flushes line-buffered output, so the log streams in real time rather than block-buffering. Quick way to catch what a host is resolving.

recipes.title: Catch SYN scans against the host
recipes.details: Shows just the SYN packets — a flood of these from one source to many ports is the classic port-scan signature.

recipes.title: Inspect cleartext HTTP
recipes.details: The famous BPF filter that drops empty TCP segments and shows only HTTP frames with payload. -A prints the ASCII so you can read requests and responses inline.

tips: Always use -nn during incidents — name resolution against a hostile capture leaks the analyst's IP back to the attacker.
Run the capture as a service account, not root: open the handle with capabilities (CAP_NET_RAW, CAP_NET_ADMIN) or use -Z to drop privileges after starting.
On Linux, -i any captures across all interfaces but the resulting pcap uses the Linux "cooked" header (SLL) — fine for analysis, but note the link-layer is different.
Combine with mbuffer or pv to stream a capture over SSH for live remote analysis: ssh box 'tcpdump -w - -U not port 22' | wireshark -k -i -
Filters are evaluated kernel-side by BPF — keep them tight to avoid copying every packet to userspace on a busy host.
Pcap files written by tcpdump are the same format Wireshark uses — interchangeable, no conversion needed.
limitations: Captures only what reaches the host's interface — switched networks need a SPAN port or a TAP for off-box visibility.
Decoding capability is far thinner than Wireshark's — for protocol drill-down you'll save a pcap and open it elsewhere.
TLS payloads are encrypted on the wire — tcpdump shows the handshake (SNI, cert) but not the application data.
On 10G+ links, software capture starts dropping packets; consider AF_PACKET v3, PF_RING, or hardware offload (Napatech, Endace).
Wire-rate truncation: an incorrectly small -s value will silently chop payloads and break reassembly downstream.
